ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general wheat flour class (FOODON_03301710); no specific high-protein variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E covers wheat flour broadly; no dedicated high-protein sub-code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 412071004 covers wheat flour as a substance; no high-protein variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of high-protein white wheat flour used in baking is approximately 120 g (about 1 cup sifted), as used for bread or pizza dough preparation.

nutrient_source.title: White wheat flour, high-protein — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05040015 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for wheat flour, bread (NDB 20081) which reports saturated fat ~0.25 g/100g; value adopted as 0.3 g/100g (rounded) consistent with HPB macro totals.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists specifically for high-protein white wheat flour in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest defensible analogous entry is white wheat flour / bread flour with GI ~71 (glucose reference), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white flour. Higher protein content (11.7 g vs ~10 g in standard white flour) may modestly lower the effective GI, but the difference is not quantified in peer-reviewed literature; GI 71 is therefore retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 69.6 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 59.3 for a 120 g serving (83.5 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg/100g) and very low sugar (1.8 g/100g) as a raw ingredient
hypertension.guidance: High-protein white wheat flour is very low in sodium (3 mg/100g) and does not trigger hypertension hard gates as a raw ingredient; however, sodium content of finished baked goods depends heavily on added salt and leavening agents, so patients with hypertension should monitor total sodium in recipes rather than the flour alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (69.6 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.5 g/100g) and high GI (~71)
type2_diabetes.guidance: High-protein white wheat flour has a high glycemic index (~71) and a very high glycemic load per serving (~59), driven by 69.6 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g with only 2.5 g dietary fibre; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion sizes, substitute with wholemeal or high-fibre flour variants, and combine with high-fibre ingredients to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate dominance raises triglycerides and LDL risk; low saturated fat and zero cholesterol are mitigating factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While high-protein white wheat flour is low in saturated fat (0.3 g/100g) and cholesterol-free, its high refined carbohydrate content (69.6 g/100g) and high glycemic load can elevate postprandial triglycerides and contribute to cardiovascular risk; patients with cardiovascular disease are advised to prefer wholemeal or high-fibre flour alternatives and to limit total refined carbohydrate intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic load promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: High-protein white wheat flour is a refined carbohydrate with a high glycaemic load that promotes formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and pro-inflammatory cytokines; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace it with wholemeal, spelt, or other high-fibre flour variants and ensure dietary anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols, adequate fibre) are obtained from other food sources.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White wheat flour, high-protein (also known as bread flour or strong white flour) is a refined milled flour produced from white wheat with a protein content of approximately 11.7 g per 100 g, significantly higher than standard all-purpose white flour (~10 g). The elevated protein — primarily gluten-forming glutenin and gliadin — gives doughs made from this flour superior elasticity, extensibility, and gas-retention capacity, making it the preferred choice for yeast-leavened breads, pizza bases, bagels, and other products requiring a strong gluten network. The flour is fine-textured, bright white in colour, and has a mild, neutral wheat flavour. Nutritionally, it provides 350 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient at 69.6 g/100g, fat at 1.7 g/100g, and dietary fibre at only 2.5 g/100g. Sodium is negligible at 3 mg/100g. The glycemic index is high (~71, glucose reference), and the glycemic load per standard baking serving (120 g) is approximately 59, making it a high-glycaemic-load ingredient. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or those managing blood glucose due to its refined carbohydrate dominance and low fibre content. It is low in sodium and therefore not a direct concern for hypertension as a raw ingredient, though finished baked goods may contain substantial added sodium. Its refined carbohydrate profile raises cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation. It contains gluten and wheat allergens and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. It is vegan, vegetarian, halal, and kosher. Wholemeal or high-fibre flour variants are recommended as substitutes for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Input record sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat and minor fatty acid values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20081, wheat flour bread). GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 as an analogous estimate for white wheat flour.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.3 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.2 g/100g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.7 g/100g) are not reported in the HPB source; values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for wheat flour, bread (NDB 20081) and adopted as best available estimates. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this specific high-protein variant are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026; USDA SR Legacy provides partial data for generic bread flour but not all fields are directly mappable. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for unfortified milled wheat flour per USDA SR Legacy. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-derived flour product. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in unfortified white wheat flour. 4. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat flour / bread flour entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct measurement for this specific high-protein variant was found. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 120 g serving: GL = (71 × 83.52) / 100 ≈ 59.3. 6. This food is a generic raw ingredient; no cuisine-specific multilingual names are required or applicable. Local name fields are null by design. 7. Ontology codes use closest defensible matches; no dedicated high-protein wheat flour codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT catalogues as of 2026.
